notes.anchor: the Nones of December always on his tongue
notes.note: 5 December 63 BC, the day Cicero as consul ordered the execution of the arrested Catilinarian conspirators without trial; Cicero was famous for alluding to this act throughout his career, and Brutus here accuses him of the same self-congratulatory repetition he charges against others.

notes.anchor: he killed more than one man and must confess himself an assassin first before he can fling the charge at Casca
notes.note: Publius Servilius Casca was the conspirator who struck the first blow at Julius Caesar on the Ides of March; Brutus accuses Cicero of hypocrisy for attacking Casca, since Cicero himself arranged extrajudicial killings of the Catilinarians and cannot consistently condemn political violence.

notes.anchor: in the matter of Casca he is imitating Bestia
notes.note: Lucius Calpurnius Bestia was a tribune of the plebs in 62 BC who attacked Cicero over the Catilinarian executions, even though Bestia himself had supported Catiline; Brutus draws the parallel that Cicero attacking Casca for killing Caesar is as two-faced as Bestia attacking Cicero for killing Catilinarians.

notes.anchor: Bestia and Clodius were to reproach his consulship
notes.note: Publius Clodius Pulcher, the populist tribune of 58 BC who passed the law sending Cicero into exile, was Cicero's bitterest enemy; Clodius made political capital of the unconstitutional execution of the Catilinarians throughout his career until his death in 52 BC.

notes.anchor: civilian and in the toga, he has held off Antony's war
notes.note: Cicero's boast in his Philippics that he, a civilian ex-magistrate (togatus), masterminded the senatorial coalition against Antony — raising Octavian's army, rallying the consuls, and passing the decrees — without himself commanding troops; Brutus retorts that the fruits of this strategy are catastrophically wrong.

notes.anchor: What is the difference between him and Salvidienus?
notes.note: Quintus Salvidienus Rufus was one of Octavian's most powerful generals and closest friends in 43 BC, of low birth but enormous military capacity; Brutus's comparison implies that Cicero has become as much an instrument of Octavian's ambition as Octavian's own military henchman.

notes.anchor: Philippus who has granted less to a stepson than Cicero is granting to a stranger
notes.note: Lucius Marcius Philippus (cos. suff. 38 BC) was Octavian's stepfather, having married Atia (Octavian's mother) after Gaius Octavius died; Brutus's thrust is that even the stepfather — with a familial obligation — was less obsequious toward Octavian than Cicero, who has no blood tie whatsoever.

notes.anchor: what terms have been arranged for our Attica
notes.note: Attica (Caecilia Attica, born c. 51 BC) was Atticus's daughter; at some point in 43–42 BC she became betrothed or married to Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa, Octavian's leading general. Brutus's inquiry about "terms" likely refers to her betrothal or dowry negotiations.

Porcia was Brutus's wife, daughter of Cato Uticensis and widow of Gaius Calpurnius Bibulus; she reportedly died in 43 BC (ancient sources disagree on the date and manner), and the concern for her health expressed here may reflect her final illness.
